title: Pricing in the New Year: Why Inflation Behaves Differently at the Start of the Year
summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of Boston research paper examines why January inflation has been notably elevated compared to other months during 2023-2025. The authors identify three contributing factors: residual seasonality in CPI data that is not fully removed by seasonal adjustment, significantly higher frequency of price changes in January (over 28%) versus other months, and compositional effects where business sectors that change prices most frequently at the start of the year also experienced higher inflation.
key_points: January monthly inflation was elevated compared to other months in each year from 2023 through 2025
Residual seasonality in CPI seasonal adjustment contributes to higher January readings: average January inflation rate of 0.23% versus 0.20% for all months
Price change frequency in January exceeds 28% of prices, significantly higher than other months, based on BLS microdata 1998-2024
During elevated inflation periods like 2023-2025, higher price change frequency amplifies inflation because prices rise more when they change
Sectors with the strongest tendency to change prices in January (medical goods/services) also experienced relatively higher inflation in recent years
Compositional effects (which sectors change prices in a given month) were stronger in 2023-2025 than in 1998-2022
why_it_matters: Understanding January inflation seasonality is important for accurately interpreting CPI releases and underlying inflation dynamics. Policymakers and analysts monitoring inflation trends need to account for these documented seasonal patterns when evaluating January CPI data, including the January 2026 CPI release on February 11, 2026.
macro_context: The paper addresses inflation measurement dynamics relevant to interpreting monthly CPI releases. It documents that seasonal patterns in January inflation were stronger in 2023-2025 than in the earlier 1998-2022 period, which has implications for understanding underlying versus seasonal inflation trends. The research is authored by Federal Reserve economists but represents their independent analysis.
market_structure_context: The paper examines price-setting behavior across business sectors using BLS price microdata, finding that certain sectors (notably medical goods and services) have a pronounced tendency to adjust prices at the start of the calendar year, which affects aggregate inflation measurement. The analysis covers 1998-2024 and uses confidential BLS microdata underlying the CPI.

issues_found: Market impact section overstates source content: claim that higher January inflation 'may have temporarily affected market expectations around Fed policy tightening or easing cycles' is not stated in source
Asset classes 'bonds' and 'fx' are not mentioned as affected markets in the source
directional_bias: neutral is not supported by source analysis
confidence: 5 for market_impact is not derivable from source - paper focuses on measurement methodology, not market price reactions
event_type is 'research_paper' but source is classified as 'data_release' in metadata - inconsistent classification

human_review.notes: The record's core factual content about January inflation seasonality is well-supported by the source and shows strong methodology (BLS microdata, regression analysis). However, the market_impact section contains interpretive claims that go beyond what the source actually states - the paper analyzes inflation measurement dynamics but does not claim these affected market expectations or Fed policy cycles. The inflated market_impact claims with moderate confidence ratings suggest possible overreach. Human review recommended to assess whether market_impact section should be removed or significantly toned down.

notes: Source is a high-quality Federal Reserve research publication with clear methodology and data citations. Content is directly relevant to macro catalysts through its analysis of inflation measurement and seasonal factors that affect monthly CPI releases. The source explicitly states this is relevant to markets and policymakers watching for changes in underlying inflation. Note: This is dated February 2026 and references the upcoming January 2026 CPI release on February 11, 2026, making it a forward-looking research context for inflation data interpretation.
